aliases.name: Psi
aliases.meaning: Greek letter ψ used as the field's umbrella term

aliases.name: Anomalous cognition / anomalous perturbation
aliases.meaning: Stargate-era and later technical terms for psi

description.summary: Psychic phenomena (psi) are alleged mental abilities — telepathy, clairvoyance, precognition, and psychokinesis — that operate outside known sensory channels and physical mechanisms. The mainstream scientific community classifies the field as pseudoscience.
description.full: Psychic phenomena, collectively designated psi (ψ) in parapsychology, are alleged human capacities to acquire information or influence matter without using known sensory or physical channels. The principal reported modalities are extrasensory perception (ESP) — including telepathy, clairvoyance, and precognition — and psychokinesis (PK), the mental influence of physical systems. The field of psychical research was institutionalised with the founding of the Society for Psychical Research (London, 1882) and the American Society for Psychical Research (Boston, 1885). Joseph Banks Rhine's Duke University laboratory (1930s onwards) introduced forced-choice card protocols and popularised the term 'parapsychology'. From the 1970s, Charles Honorton's ganzfeld experiments and Russell Targ and Harold Puthoff's Stanford Research Institute remote-viewing program were central; the U.S. government's Stargate Project ran from 1972 to 1995. Mainstream scientific consensus, formalised by reviews including Hyman and Honorton's joint communiqué (1986), the AIR review of Stargate (Mumford et al. 1995), and the Wiseman / French / Ritchie failed replications of Bem (2012), is that psi has no replicable, accepted experimental basis and is best classed as pseudoscience. The Parapsychological Association is an affiliate of the American Association for the Advancement of Science, but the field rarely publishes in mainstream scientific journals.

etymology.origin: From Greek psyche (soul, mind); the umbrella term 'psi' was introduced by B. P. Wiesner and Robert Thouless in 1942
etymology.meaning: Phenomena of the mind/soul outside the known senses
etymology.first_use: Wiesner and Thouless (1942) for 'psi'; 'psychical research' attested in English from the 1880s; 'parapsychology' popularised by Rhine in the 1930s

related_phenomena.name: Clairvoyance
related_phenomena.description: Direct perception of distant or hidden contemporaneous events

physical.morphology: Psychic phenomena are alleged cognitive and motor abilities rather than physical entities; they are characterised in terms of experimental protocols and reported experiences.

abilities.description: Mind-to-mind communication of thoughts, images, or feelings
Acquisition of information about distant or hidden contemporaneous targets
Acquisition of information about future events
Mental influence of macroscopic objects, or of stochastic systems such as random-event generators (micro-PK)
Free-response protocol for describing distant geographic targets, developed at SRI from the 1970s
Non-conscious physiological responses (e.g. skin conductance) to future stimuli

patterns: Reported in spontaneous and laboratory contexts
Frequently reported in dreams, altered states, and emotionally charged events
Subject to extensive critique on methodological and statistical grounds
Effect sizes in laboratory studies are small and not reliably replicable

range.description: Belief in psi is documented across most cultures and historical periods. Laboratory study has been concentrated in the United States, the United Kingdom, Western Europe, and the former Soviet Union.

hotspots.name: Society for Psychical Research, London
hotspots.notes: Oldest organised body for the study of psychic phenomena.

hotspots.name: Duke University Parapsychology Laboratory
hotspots.notes: Rhine's flagship laboratory; focus of forced-choice ESP and PK research.

hotspots.name: Stanford Research Institute (SRI)
hotspots.notes: Site of Targ and Puthoff's remote-viewing research and the early Stargate Project.

hotspots.name: Princeton Engineering Anomalies Research (PEAR)
hotspots.notes: Robert Jahn's PEAR lab researched human-machine interaction and PK with random-event generators.

first_recorded.source: Cross-cultural folklore of telepathy, prophecy, and divination

peak_activity_periods.description: Foundational era of psychical research (SPR, ASPR)
Remote viewing and the U.S. Stargate Project
Ganzfeld debates, Bem (2011) episode, and the replication-crisis era

origins.cultural_roots: Psychic-phenomena claims appear across human cultures in folklore (second sight, prophecy, spirit communication) and in many religious and philosophical traditions.
origins.earliest_accounts: Hellenistic, Hebrew, Indian, and Chinese sources record claims of telepathy, prophecy, and miraculous influence over matter; modern systematic study begins in the 19th century with the Spiritualist movement and psychical research.
origins.folklore_connections: Closely connected to traditions of mediumship, prophecy, divination, ghost lore, and miraculous saints across many religions.

timeline.event: Society for Psychical Research (SPR) founded in London by Henry Sidgwick, Frederic Myers, and Edmund Gurney
American Society for Psychical Research founded in Boston
timeline.source: Wikipedia: American Society for Psychical Research

timeline.event: J. B. Rhine publishes Extra-Sensory Perception, marshalling Duke Zener-card data
B. P. Wiesner and Robert Thouless propose 'psi' as a neutral umbrella term
Parapsychological Association founded; admitted as AAAS affiliate in 1969
timeline.significance: Institutionalisation of parapsychology in the U.S. scientific community

timeline.event: Targ and Puthoff begin remote-viewing experiments at SRI, leading to the U.S. Stargate Project
timeline.significance: Most-funded program of psi research in history; ran until 1995

timeline.event: First published ganzfeld telepathy experiment by Charles Honorton
timeline.significance: Beginning of the most extensively meta-analysed ESP protocol

timeline.event: Princeton Engineering Anomalies Research (PEAR) lab established by Robert Jahn
timeline.significance: PK and human-machine interaction research; closed 2007

timeline.event: Hyman–Honorton joint communiqué on ganzfeld methodology
timeline.significance: Key methodological reform agreement between sceptics and proponents

timeline.event: Termination of the U.S. Stargate Project; AIR review concludes operational use unjustified
timeline.significance: End of major government psi-research funding
timeline.source: Mumford, Rose & Goslin (1995), AIR Stargate review

timeline.event: Daryl Bem publishes 'Feeling the Future' in JPSP
timeline.significance: Catalyses modern replication-crisis reforms in psychology

timeline.event: Failed pre-registered replications of Bem (Ritchie, Wiseman & French; Galak et al.)
timeline.significance: Independent failure to replicate Bem's effects
timeline.source: Ritchie et al. (2012); Galak et al. (2012)

history.modern_era: Mainstream science classifies psi research as pseudoscience due to the absence of replicable effects, methodological criticism, and the lack of an accepted physical mechanism. Parapsychology continues as a niche field, primarily through the Parapsychological Association, the SPR, the Institute of Noetic Sciences, and the Rhine Research Center. The Bem (2011) episode and its failed replications were central to the broader replication crisis in psychology.

notable.name: Founding of the Society for Psychical Research
notable.description: Founding of the Society for Psychical Research in London, the first organised body devoted to systematic study of psychic phenomena and Spiritualist claims.

credibility.notes: Foundational event for the modern study of psi.

notable.name: SRI remote-viewing program launched

location.description: Stanford Research Institute, Menlo Park, California

notable.description: Russell Targ and Harold Puthoff began remote-viewing experiments at SRI under U.S. government sponsorship, eventually feeding into the Stargate Project (1972–1995).

factors: AIR (1995) review found operational utility unjustified
Methodological criticism from Ray Hyman and others
credibility.notes: Most-funded psi program in history; ended without operational acceptance.

sources: Mumford, Rose & Goslin (1995), AIR Stargate review

notable.name: Hyman–Honorton joint communiqué

location.description: Joint statement; principal authors at University of Oregon and Maimonides Medical Center

notable.description: Sceptic Ray Hyman and proponent Charles Honorton agreed in a joint communiqué that early ganzfeld experiments suffered from significant methodological problems and that future studies required automated controls.

factors: Joint document by leading sceptic and proponent
credibility.notes: Key reference for methodological reform in psi research.

sources: Hyman, R., & Honorton, C. (1986), J. Parapsychology

notable.name: Stargate Project terminated

location.description: U.S. government program; review by American Institutes for Research

notable.description: The U.S. Stargate Project, which had run remote-viewing operations since 1972, was terminated after the AIR review concluded that effects were too small for operational use and could be explained by methodological flaws.

credibility.notes: Marks the end of the most prominent state-funded psi-research program.

sources: Mumford, Rose & Goslin (1995), AIR Stargate review

documentary.name: Bem (2011) 'Feeling the Future'
documentary.description: Nine-experiment paper reporting evidence for precognition; foundational case in modern psi controversy.
documentary.analysis: Failed pre-registered replications by Ritchie, Wiseman & French (2012) and others; effects not robust.

documentary.name: AIR Stargate review
documentary.description: American Institutes for Research evaluation of the U.S. remote-viewing program.
documentary.analysis: Concluded that operational use was not justified; effects too small or unreliable for intelligence purposes.

documentary.name: Hyman–Honorton joint communiqué (1986)
documentary.description: Joint sceptic / proponent agreement on ganzfeld methodology.
documentary.analysis: Key methodological inflection point; subsequent autoganzfeld experiments addressed many but not all concerns.

paranormal.name: Genuine psi
paranormal.description: Some humans, at least sometimes, possess weak but genuine psi abilities — telepathy, clairvoyance, precognition, and PK — beyond known sensory and physical channels.

paranormal.name: Quantum-information / non-local mind hypotheses
paranormal.description: Speculative interpretations invoke quantum non-locality, retrocausality, or panpsychist consciousness frameworks to argue psi could be physically possible.
evidence_cited: Interpretive analogies to quantum entanglement and the measurement problem

skeptical.name: Pseudoscience classification
skeptical.description: Psi research, after more than a century of investigation, has produced no replicable, accepted evidence. The mainstream consensus classes parapsychology as pseudoscience.

skeptical.name: Methodological and statistical artefacts
skeptical.description: Reported psi effects are produced by sensory leakage, randomisation flaws, optional stopping, data tampering, publication bias, and questionable research practices.

skeptical.name: Cognitive biases and folklore transmission
skeptical.description: Spontaneous psi experiences are explained by coincidence, confirmation bias, false memory, and post-hoc retrofitting; cultural transmission stabilises the categories.
evidence_cited: Cognitive psychology of probability and memory

traditions: Folk traditions of the 'evil eye' and curse magic
stories: Pat Price and Ingo Swann remote-viewing legends
folklore.beliefs: Substantial fractions of the public in many countries report belief in or personal experience of psi; surveys consistently show majority belief in some form of psychic phenomenon.

literature: Phantasms of the Living — Gurney, Myers & Podmore (1886)
The Skeptic's Dictionary — Robert T. Carroll (2003)
film_tv: The Men Who Stare at Goats (2009)
other: Foundational influence on 20th-century science fiction and horror
Frequent subject of skeptical and proponent popular-press treatments

researchers.name: Dr. Charles Honorton
researchers.contribution: Pioneered ganzfeld telepathy experiments and autoganzfeld

researchers.name: Dr. Ray Hyman
researchers.contribution: Leading sceptical methodologist; co-author of the 1986 ganzfeld joint communiqué

researchers.name: Russell Targ and Dr. Harold Puthoff
Dr. Dean Radin
researchers.contribution: Meta-analytic psi research and presentiment studies

researchers.name: Dr. Chris French
researchers.contribution: Anomalistic psychology research and sceptical replication

organizations.name: Society for Psychical Research (SPR)
organizations.focus: Founded 1882; flagship journal of psychical research

organizations.name: Rhine Research Center
organizations.focus: Successor institution to Rhine's Duke laboratory

research.ongoing_studies: Pre-registered replication and meta-analysis of psi protocols (notably Bem-style precognition tasks); anomalistic psychology research; consciousness studies that situate psi claims alongside related anomalous-experience research.

sources.title: A Joint Communiqué: The Psi Ganzfeld Controversy
Feeling the Future: Experimental Evidence for Anomalous Retroactive Influences on Cognition and Affect
sources.publication: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

sources.title: Failing the Future: Three Unsuccessful Attempts to Replicate Bem's 'Retroactive Facilitation of Recall' Effect
An Evaluation of Remote Viewing: Research and Applications (Stargate Review)
The Conscious Universe: The Scientific Truth of Psychic Phenomena
In Search of the Light: The Adventures of a Parapsychologist
Give the Null Hypothesis a Chance: Reasons to Remain Doubtful about the Existence of Psi
Mind-Reach: Scientists Look at Psychic Ability
